notes: This file is a review artifact for proposed additions and alias expansions.
Suggestions are biased toward map-visible retail brands, regional banks, convenience stores, and apparel/luxury chains.
_meta.structureRule: Distinct companies, houses, subsidiaries, and map-visible consumer entities should be modeled as standalone items with parentEntityId rather than folded into parent aliases. Alias lists should mostly capture spelling, punctuation, and formatting variants.
staticFieldsFilled: domains (from official review URLs when directly inferable)
ceoName (where verified or inherited from parent/source)
remainingManualFields: ceoName for unresolved or fast-changing entries
publicFigureName for brands without a clearly associated current public figure
brand domains for child entities whose review URL is a parent corporate page
leadershipPassNotes: CEO names were filled where supported by current web checks or inherited from an existing live/source parent for split candidates.
Public figure names were added selectively for clearly associated current public figures, controlling family leaders, or founder-linked names already central to the brand identity.
Ambiguous cases, interim structures, and brand entries without a clear current figure were intentionally left blank for manual review.

currentFindings.summary: The review file now includes canonical names, inferred official domains, and unverified status defaults, while intentionally leaving FEC-owned fields alone and flagging CEO/public-figure data as a separate manual verification pass.

Real mall-directory testing still exposed concrete tenant gaps, especially outlet apparel, footwear, accessories, and luxury brands, plus one false-positive collision where Pandora jewelry was being masked by the unrelated siriusxm/Pandora alias.

Outlet-style apparel and footwear coverage was still missing a dedicated brand pass; this review file now includes standalone outlet-store brands plus child split candidates for brand banners buried inside live parent entities.
The review file now separates parent-company proposals from child-entity split candidates so distinct houses, subsidiaries, and consumer banners are not hidden inside alias lists.
Regional grocery, convenience, and bank coverage is materially thinner than national-chain coverage.
Several major apparel and luxury groups with physical stores are absent, including Kering, Capri, Tapestry, PVH, Ralph Lauren, and Fast Retailing.
The matcher is exact after normalization, so spaced, hyphenated, numeric, and misspelled variants still need explicit aliases.
A few parents already exist but still miss major map-facing banners, especially Ahold Delhaize, Kroger, LVMH, 7-Eleven, Circle K, Casey's, and travel-center chains.
LVMH exists in the live file, but its house coverage is still too shallow for luxury storefront matching; adding more maison aliases or child entities would materially improve map hits.
